questions.question: Should the export capture the full network (all markets + all conditional edges + clique data) or only a single market's perspective? The BayesNetGraph shows the entire network but is rendered on a per-market page.
questions.why_it_matters: Determines the scope of the JSON schema — whole-network export vs single-market export are very different data shapes and UI placements.

questions.question: Where do conditional edges come from? BayesNetGraph receives them as a prop (`conditionalEdges`) but MarketDetail passes none — are edges stored server-side or only exist transiently in the UI?
questions.why_it_matters: If edges are only transient/derived, the export must reconstruct them from server data or they won't appear in the export. This affects what API calls are needed during export.

questions.question: Node layout positions are computed dynamically via `layoutNodes()` (circular arrangement). Should we export computed positions or let the importer re-layout? If exporting positions, users who manually rearrange nodes (future feature) would preserve their layout.
questions.why_it_matters: Currently there's no drag-to-reposition, so exporting computed positions adds schema complexity with no current user benefit. But the task description says to include layout positions.

questions.question: On import, should the system create markets on the server (POST /v1/markets) or just load a visual-only representation? Creating real markets requires the backend to accept them and the user needs a configured session.
questions.why_it_matters: This is the fundamental question of import scope. A visual-only import is simple; actually creating server-side markets is complex and may not even be supported by the API for bulk creation with pre-set probabilities.

questions.question: Where exactly should the export/import buttons go? The BayesNetGraph panel header has a title and stats line. The MarketDetail page has no dedicated toolbar. Should buttons go in the graph panel header, or should we create a new toolbar component?
questions.why_it_matters: The task says 'graph toolbar area' but no toolbar exists today. We need to either add buttons to the existing header row in BayesNetGraph or create a new toolbar div.

questions.question: What schema validation approach should be used on import? Options: runtime validation with a hand-written validator, a library like zod/ajv, or simple type guards. The project currently has no schema validation library.
questions.why_it_matters: Adding a new dependency (zod/ajv) vs hand-rolling validation affects bundle size, maintenance, and reliability of error messages.

assumptions.assumption: Export captures the entire network state (all markets, edges, cliques) rather than a single market.
assumptions.if_wrong: Schema would be scoped to a single market's data and the UI button placement might change.

assumptions.assumption: Import is visual/read-only — it loads a snapshot for viewing, not for creating real server-side markets.
assumptions.if_wrong: Would need to implement bulk market creation via API, handle authentication, and deal with conflicts/errors for each market.

assumptions.assumption: Buttons will be added to the existing BayesNetGraph panel header (the flex row with title and stats) rather than creating a separate toolbar component.
assumptions.if_wrong: Would need a new toolbar component with its own styling, which is more work but cleaner separation.

assumptions.assumption: Hand-rolled TypeScript validation is sufficient for the JSON schema (no need for zod/ajv dependency).
assumptions.if_wrong: Would need to add a schema validation dependency and configure it.

assumptions.assumption: Layout positions will be included in the export as the task description requests, even though they're currently computed dynamically.
assumptions.if_wrong: Could simplify the schema by omitting positions, but the task explicitly mentions layout positions.

assumptions.assumption: Conditional edges are derived from engine stats or conditional edits and need to be fetched from the API during export.
assumptions.if_wrong: If edges come from a different source, the export logic would need to be adjusted.

decomposition_assessment.complexity_reason: Moderate complexity: ~3-4 files to create/modify (JSON schema type, export logic, import logic + UI, BayesNetGraph toolbar). No architectural changes needed — it's additive. Estimated ~200-300 lines of new code. All frontend-only changes.
decomposition_assessment.uncertainty_reason: Key uncertainties around import behavior (visual-only vs server-side creation) and where conditional edges come from. However, the export side is straightforward — serialize what's already fetched. The design decisions are contained within the frontend graph feature.
independent_subproblems: Design and implement JSON schema types for network export format
Implement export logic (serialize network state + trigger download)
Implement import logic (file upload UI + parse/validate JSON + load network)
Add toolbar buttons to BayesNetGraph panel
decomposition_assessment.decision_reason: Complexity 3 and uncertainty 3 — both below the threshold of 4. While there are 4 logical sub-tasks, they're tightly coupled (schema types feed into both export and import, toolbar buttons trigger export/import) and touch only 3-4 files in the same frontend feature module. Splitting into children would add coordination overhead without meaningful parallelism benefit.
